value:
Operationalization plan for the seed-density principle. See conventions.seedDensity for the principle itself. Three layers, in increasing cost order. Don't build the later layers before validating the earlier ones — convention before infrastructure.

LAYER 1 — Bake the principle into the codex itself.
Status: DONE 2026-04-09. Saved as conventions.seedDensity. CLAUDE.md updated with a "Write seeds, not encyclopedias" section pointing at it. Future agents read it on bootstrap and write entries with the activation-per-byte lens. Side benefit: when next walking existing entries, the principle gives a sharp test ("does this seed land in terrain the LLM couldn't have reached?") that surfaces entries to rewrite or delete.

LAYER 2 — Build tools that operationalize it.
File these as v1.12+ GitHub issues. Don't build until Layer 1 has shaped a few sessions of real entry writing — validate the principle is actually changing how entries get authored before investing in tooling.

  A. ccli lint --seed-quality
  Heuristic detection of low-amplification entries. Flag: entries with no project-specific terms, entries that are pure restatement of common knowledge, entries shorter than X bytes with no references to other entries or files. Output: "consider rewriting" warnings, not errors. Makes the principle enforceable as a soft check. Implementable in ~100 lines. The hard part is defining the heuristics, not the lint plumbing — it could lean on existing arch.lint infrastructure.

  B. ccli topology (or ccli graph)
  Co-occurrence analysis on audit.jsonl. Surface which entries get pulled in the same sessions. Output: ranked list of biome adjacencies, optionally a graphviz DOT file. Turns the audit log from a debugging tool into a knowledge-discovery tool. Implementable in ~200 lines. Likely the killer screenshot for the dogfooding post — visualize the implicit project topology nobody wrote down explicitly.

LAYER 3 — North-star ideas (v1.13+ and beyond).
Lens for prioritizing future features: "does this help users write seeds that activate denser biomes, or does it just add storage capacity?" Examples that pass the test:

  - Telemetry-driven tier promotion in codex_context (hot entries auto-promote to essential tier based on real usage instead of static rules)
  - Seed-pack templates for ccli init (curated entry sets for common project shapes: node-typescript, python-fastapi, rust-cli, etc.)
  - Optional meta.testedWith: <model-id> annotations to track generator-version drift (entries written for older models may activate differently in newer ones)
  - ccli explain <entry> — ask the LLM what biome the entry is selecting; basically a self-introspection tool that helps the user understand what context their entries actually unlock

None of these should ship soon. They're a north star, not a roadmap. They help filter feature ideas, not generate sprint backlogs.

WHAT NOT TO DO:
- Don't try to directly measure LLM activation. Too expensive, brittle, model-specific. Heuristics over measurement.
- Don't make seed-density a hard requirement. It's a principle, not a rule. Some entries (literal commands, version pins) are necessarily low-amplification and that's fine.
- Don't build Layer 2 before Layer 1 has shaped a few sessions of real writing. Convention before infrastructure (same wisdom as the session.last MVP).
- Don't promise the seed framing is a proven model. It's a useful reframing, not a research result. After a week of using it, parts may feel forced. That's fine — iterate or drop those parts.

ORIGIN:
Emerged from a stream-of-consciousness conversation 2026-04-09 (see the session that produced the v1.11.1 release validation work). User started with an LLM-weights/matrix-multiplication framing, refined it to Minecraft seeds (small input + generator → large structured output). The seed metaphor breaks the Shannon-smuggling objection cleanly: codex bytes don't store extra information, they activate context already living in the LLM's pretrained weights. The reframing suggests writing entries optimized for amplification per byte, not completeness — which in turn suggests a series of features (lint, topology, tier promotion) that operationalize the principle.
